text: In the third year of the reign of Joakim, king of Juda, Nabuchodonosor, king of Babylon, came to Jerusalem, and besieged it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Lord delivered into his hands Joakim, the king of Juda, and part of the vessels of the house of God: and he carried them away into the land of Sennaar, to the house of his god, and the vessels he brought into the treasure house of his god.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king spoke to Asphenez, the master of the eunuchs, that he should bring in some of the children of Israel, and of the king’s seed, and of the princes,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Children in whom there was no blemish, well favoured, and skilful in all wisdom, acute in knowledge, and instructed in science, and such as might stand in the king’s palace, that he might teach them the learning, and tongue of the Chaldeans.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king appointed them a daily provision, of his own meat, and of the wine of which he drank himself, that being nourished three years, afterwards they might stand before the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there were among them of the children of Juda, Daniel, Ananias, Misael, and Azarias.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the master of the eunuchs gave them names: to Daniel, Baltassar: to Ananias, Sidrach: to Misael, Misach: and to Azarias, Abdenago.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But Daniel purposed in his heart that he would not be defiled with the king’s table, nor with the wine which he drank: and he requested the master of the eunuchs that he might not be defiled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And God gave to Daniel grace and mercy in the sight of the prince of the eunuchs.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the prince of the eunuchs said to Daniel: I fear my lord, the king, who hath appointed you meat and drink: who if he should see your faces leaner than those of the other youths, your equals, you shall endanger my head to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Daniel said to Malasar, whom the prince of the eunuchs had appointed over Daniel, Ananias, Misael, and Azarias:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Try, I beseech thee, thy servants for ten days, and let pulse be given us to eat, and water to drink:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And look upon our faces, and the faces of the children that eat of the king’s meat: and as thou shalt see, deal with thy servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he had heard these words, he tried them for ten days.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after ten days, their faces appeared fairer and fatter than all the children that ate of the king’s meat.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So Malasar took their portions, and the wine that they should drink: and he gave them pulse.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And to these children God gave knowledge, and understanding in every book, and wisdom: but to Daniel the understanding also of all visions and dreams.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the days were ended, after which the king had ordered they should be brought in: the prince of the eunuchs brought them in before Nabuchodonosor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when the king had spoken to them, there were not found among them all such as Daniel, Ananias, Misael, and Azarias: and they stood in the king’s presence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And in all matters of wisdom and understanding, that the king inquired of them, he found them ten times better than all the diviners, and wise men, that were in all his kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Daniel continued even to the first year of king Cyrus.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the second year of the reign of Nabuchodonosor, Nabuchodonosor had a dream, and his spirit was terrified, and his dream went out of his mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king commanded to call together the diviners and the wise men, and the magicians, and the Chaldeans: to declare to the king his dreams: so they came and stood before the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king said to them: I saw a dream: and being troubled in mind I know not what I saw.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the Chaldeans answered the king in Syriac: O king, live for ever: tell to thy servants thy dream, and we will declare the interpretation thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king, answering, said to the Chaldeans: The thing is gone out of my mind: unless you tell me the dream, and the meaning thereof, you shall be put to death, and your houses shall be confiscated.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: but if you tell the dream, and the meaning of it, you shall receive of me rewards, and gifts, and great honour: therefore, tell me the dream, and the interpretation thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They answered again and said: Let the king tell his servants the dream, and we will declare the interpretation of it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king answered and said: I know for certain, that you seek to gain time, since you know that the thing is gone from me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If, therefore, you tell me not the dream, there is one sentence concerning you, that you have also framed a lying interpretation, and full of deceit, to speak before me till the time pass away. Tell me, therefore, the dream, that I may know that you also give a true interpretation thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the Chaldeans answered before the king, and said: There is no man upon earth, that can accomplish thy word, O king; neither doth any king, though great and mighty, ask such a thing of any diviner, or wise man, or Chaldean.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the thing that thou askest, O king, is difficult: nor can any one be found that can shew it before the king, except the gods, whose conversation is not with men.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Upon hearing this, the king in fury, and in great wrath, commanded that all the wise men of Babylon should be put to death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the decree being gone forth, the wise men were slain: and Daniel and his companions were sought for, to be put to death.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Daniel inquired concerning the law and the sentence, of Arioch, the general of the king’s army, who was gone forth to kill the wise men of Babylon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he asked him that had received the orders of the king, why so cruel a sentence was gone forth from the face of the king. And when Arioch had told the matter to Daniel,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Daniel went in, and desired of the king, that he would give him time to resolve the question, and declare it to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went into his house, and told the matter to Ananias, and Misael, and Azarias, his companions:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To the end that they should ask mercy at the face of the God of heaven, concerning this secret, and that Daniel and his companions might not perish with the rest of the wise men of Babylon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then was the mystery revealed to Daniel by a vision in the night: and Daniel blessed the God of heaven,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And speaking, he said: Blessed be the name of the Lord from eternity and for evermore: for wisdom and fortitude are his.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he changeth times and ages: taketh away kingdoms, and establisheth them: giveth wisdom to the wise, and knowledge to them that have understanding:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He revealeth deep and hidden things, and knoweth what is in darkness: and light is with him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To thee, O God of our fathers, I give thanks, and I praise thee: because thou hast given me wisdom and strength: and now thou hast shewn me what we desired of thee, for thou hast made known to us the king’s discourse.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this Daniel went in to Arioch, to whom the king had given orders to destroy the wise men of Babylon, and he spoke thus to him: Destroy not the wise men of Babylon: bring me in before the king, and I will tell the solution to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Arioch in haste brought in Daniel to the king, and said to him: I have found a man of the children of the captivity of Juda, that will resolve the question to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The king answered, and said to Daniel, whose name was Baltassar: Thinkest thou indeed that thou canst tell me the dream that I saw, and the interpretation thereof?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Daniel made answer before the king, and said: The secret that the king desireth to know, none of the wise men, or the philosophers, or the diviners, or the soothsayers, can declare to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But there is a God in heaven that revealeth mysteries, who hath shewn to thee, O king Nabuchodonosor, what is to come to pass in the latter times. Thy dream, and the visions of thy head upon thy bed, are these:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou, O king, didst begin to think in thy bed, what should come to pass hereafter: and he that revealeth mysteries shewed thee what shall come to pass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To me also this secret is revealed, not by any wisdom that I have more than all men alive: but that the interpretation might be made manifest to the king, and thou mightest know the thoughts of thy mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou, O king, sawest, and behold there was as it were a great statue: this statue, which was great and high, tall of stature, stood before thee, and the look thereof was terrible.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The head of this statue was of fine gold, but the breast and the arms of silver, and the belly and the thighs of brass.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the legs of iron, the feet part of iron and part of clay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thus thou sawest, till a stone was cut out of a mountain without hands: and it struck the statue upon the feet thereof that were of iron and clay, and broke them in pieces.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then was the iron, the clay, the brass, the silver, and the gold broken to pieces together, and became like the chaff of a summer’s threshing floor, and they were carried away by the wind: and there was no place found for them: but the stone that struck the statue became a great mountain, and filled the whole earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This is the dream: we will also tell the interpretation thereof before thee, O king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou art a king of kings: and the God of heaven hath given thee a kingdom, and strength, and power, and glory:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all places wherein the children of men, and the beasts of the field do dwell: he hath also given the birds of the air into thy hand, and hath put all things under thy power: thou, therefore, art the head of gold.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after thee shall rise up another kingdom, inferior to thee, of silver: and another third kingdom of brass, which shall rule over all the world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the fourth kingdom shall be as iron. As iron breaketh into pieces, and subdueth all things, so shall that break, and destroy all these.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas thou sawest the feet, and the toes, part of potter’s clay, and part of iron: the kingdom shall be divided, but yet it shall take its origin from the iron, according as thou sawest the iron mixed with the miry clay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And as the toes of the feet were part of iron, and part of clay: the kingdom shall be partly strong, and partly broken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas thou sawest the iron mixed with miry clay, they shall be mingled indeed together with the seed of man, but they shall not stick fast one to another, as iron cannot be mixed with clay.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But in the days of those kingdoms, the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed, and his kingdom shall not be delivered up to another people: and it shall break in pieces, and shall consume all these kingdoms: and itself shall stand for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: According as thou sawest, that the stone was cut out of the mountain without hands, and broke in pieces the clay and the iron, and the brass, and the silver, and the gold, the great God hath shewn the king what shall come to pass hereafter, and the dream is true, and the interpretation thereof is faithful.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then king Nabuchodonosor fell on his face, and worshipped Daniel, and commanded that they should offer in sacrifice to him victims and incense.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king spoke to Daniel, and said: Verily, your God is the God of gods, and Lord of kings, and a revealer of hidden things: seeing thou couldst discover this secret.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king advanced Daniel to a high station, and gave him many and great gifts: and he made him governor over all the provinces of Babylon: and chief of the magistrates over all the wise men of Babylon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Daniel requested of the king, and he appointed Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, over the works of the province of Babylon: but Daniel himself was in the king’s palace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: King Nabuchodonosor made a statue of gold, of sixty cubits high, and six cubits broad, and he set it up in the plain of Dura, of the province of Babylon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Nabuchodonosor, the king, sent to call together the nobles, the magistrates, and the judges, the captains, the rulers, and governors, and all the chief men of the provinces, to come to the dedication of the statue which king Nabuchodonosor had set up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the nobles, the magistrates, and the judges, the captains, and rulers, and the great men that were placed in authority, and all the princes of the provinces, were gathered together to come to the dedication of the statue, which king Nabuchodonosor had set up. And they stood before the statue which king Nabuchodonosor had set up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then a herald cried with a strong voice: To you it is commanded, O nations, tribes and languages:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That in the hour that you shall hear the sound of the trumpet, and of the flute, and of the harp, of the sackbut, and of the psaltery, and of the symphony, and of all kind of music, ye fall down and adore the golden statue which king Nabuchodonosor hath set up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if any man shall not fall down and adore, he shall the same hour be cast into a furnace of burning fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Upon this, therefore, at the time when all the people heard the sound of the trumpet, the flute, and the harp, of the sackbut, and the psaltery, of the symphony, and of all kind of music, all the nations, tribes, and languages fell down and adored the golden statue which king Nabuchodonosor had set up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And presently at that very time some Chaldeans came and accused the Jews,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And said to king Nabuchodonosor: O king, live for ever:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou, O king, hast made a decree, that every man that shall hear the sound of the trumpet, the flute, and the harp, of the sackbut, and the psaltery, of the symphony, and of all kind of music, shall prostrate himself, and adore the golden statue:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that if any man shall not fall down and adore, he should be cast into a furnace of burning fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there are certain Jews, whom thou hast set over the works of the province of Babylon, Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago: these men, O king, have slighted thy decree: they worship not thy gods, nor do they adore the golden statue which thou hast set up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Nabuchodonosor in fury, and in wrath, commanded that Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago should be brought: who immediately were brought before the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Nabuchodonosor, the king, spoke to them, and said: Is it true, O Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, that you do not worship my gods, nor adore the golden statue that I have set up?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, if you be ready, at what hour soever, you shall hear the sound of the trumpet, flute, harp, sackbut, and psaltery, and symphony, and of all kind of music, prostrate yourselves, and adore the statue which I have made: but if you do not adore, you shall be cast the same hour into the furnace of burning fire: and who is the God that shall deliver you out of my hand?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, answered, and said to king Nabuchodonosor: We have no occasion to answer thee concerning this matter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For behold our God, whom we worship, is able to save us from the furnace of burning fire, and to deliver us out of thy hands, O king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But if he will not, be it known to thee, O king, that we will not worship thy gods, nor adore the golden statue which thou hast set up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then was Nabuchodonosor filled with fury: and the countenance of his face was changed against Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, and he commanded that the furnace should be heated seven times more than it had been accustomed to be heated.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he commanded the strongest men that were in his army, to bind the feet of Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, and to cast them into the furnace of burning fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And immediately these men were bound, and were cast into the furnace of burning fire, with their coats, and their caps, and their shoes, and their garments.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the king’s commandment was urgent, and the furnace was heated exceedingly. And the flame of the fire slew those men that had cast in Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But these three men, that is, Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, fell down bound in the midst of the furnace of burning fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they walked in the midst of the flame, praising God, and blessing the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Azarias standing up, prayed in this manner, and opening his mouth in the midst of the fire, he said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed art thou, O Lord, the God of our fathers, and thy name is worthy of praise, and glorious for ever:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou art just in all that thou hast done to us, and all thy works are true, and thy ways right, and all thy judgments true.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thou hast executed true judgments in all the things that thou hast brought upon us, and upon Jerusalem, the holy city of our fathers: for according to truth and judgment, thou hast brought all these things upon us for our sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For we have sinned, and committed iniquity, departing from thee: and we have trespassed in all things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And we have not hearkened to thy commandments, nor have we observed nor done as thou hadst commanded us, that it might go well with us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, all that thou hast brought upon us, and every thing that thou hast done to us, thou hast done in true judgment:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thou hast delivered us into the hands of our enemies that are unjust, and most wicked, and prevaricators, and to a king unjust, and most wicked beyond all that are upon the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now we cannot open our mouths: we are become a shame, and a reproach to thy servants, and to them that worship thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Deliver us not up for ever, we beseech thee, for thy name’s sake, and abolish not thy covenant.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And take not away thy mercy from us, for the sake of Abraham, thy beloved, and Isaac, thy servant, and Israel, thy holy one:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To whom thou hast spoken, promising that thou wouldst multiply their seed as the stars of heaven, and as the sand that is on the sea shore.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For we, O Lord, are diminished more than any nation, and are brought low in all the earth this day for our sins.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Neither is there at this time prince, or leader, or prophet, or holocaust, or sacrifice, or oblation, or incense, or place of first fruits before thee,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That we may find thy mercy: nevertheless, in a contrite heart and humble spirit let us be accepted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As in holocausts of rams, and bullocks, and as in thousands of fat lambs: so let our sacrifice be made in thy sight this day, that it may please thee: for there is no confusion to them that trust in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now we follow thee with all our heart, and we fear thee, and seek thy face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Put us not to confusion, but deal with us according to thy meekness, and according to the multitude of thy mercies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And deliver us, according to thy wonderful works, and give glory to thy name, O Lord:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let all them be confounded that shew evils to thy servants, let them be confounded in all thy might, and let their strength be broken:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And let them know that thou art the Lord, the only God, and glorious over all the world.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now the king’s servants that had cast them in, ceased not to heat the furnace with brimstone and tow, and pitch, and dry sticks,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the flame mounted up above the furnace nine and forty cubits:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it broke forth, and burnt such of the Chaldeans as it found near the furnace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the angel of the Lord went down with Azarias and his companions into the furnace: and he drove the flame of the fire out of the furnace,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And made the midst of the furnace like the blowing of a wind bringing dew, and the fire touched them not at all, nor troubled them, nor did them any harm.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then these three, as with one mouth, praised and glorified and blessed God, in the furnace, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed art thou, O Lord, the God of our fathers; and worthy to be praised, and glorified, and exalted above all for ever: and blessed is the holy name of thy glory: and worthy to be praised and exalted above all, in all ages.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed art thou in the holy temple of thy glory: and exceedingly to be praised and exalted above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed art thou on the throne of thy kingdom, and exceedingly to be praised and exalted above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed art thou that beholdest the depths, and sittest upon the cherubims: and worthy to be praised and exalted above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed art thou in the firmament of heaven: and worthy of praise, and glorious for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All ye works of the Lord, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye angels of the Lord, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye heavens, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O all ye waters that are above the heavens, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O all ye powers of the Lord, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye sun and moon, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye stars of heaven, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O every shower and dew, bless ye the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O all ye spirits of God, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye fire and heat, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye cold and heat, bless the Lord, praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye dews and hoar frost, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye frost and cold, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye ice and snow, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye nights and days, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye light and darkness, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye lightnings and clouds, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O let the earth bless the Lord: let it praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye mountains and hills, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O all ye things that spring up in the earth, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye fountains, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye seas and rivers, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye whales, and all that move in the waters, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O all ye fowls of the air, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O all ye beasts and cattle, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye sons of men, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O let Israel bless the Lord: let them praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye priests of the Lord, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye servants of the Lord, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye spirits and souls of the just, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O ye holy and humble of heart, bless the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O Ananias, Azarias, and Misael, bless ye the Lord: praise and exalt him above all for ever. For he hath delivered us from hell, and saved us out of the hand of death, and delivered us out of the midst of the burning flame, and saved us out of the midst of the fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O give thanks to the Lord, because he is good: because his mercy endureth for ever and ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O all ye religious, bless the Lord, the God of gods: praise him, and give him thanks, because his mercy endureth for ever and ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Nabuchodonosor, the king, was astonished, and rose up in haste, and said to his nobles: Did we not cast three men bound into the midst of the fire? They answered the king, and said: True, O king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He answered, and said: Behold, I see four men loose, and walking in the midst of the fire, and there is no hurt in them, and the form of the fourth is like the son of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Nabuchodonosor came to the door of the burning fiery furnace, and said: Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, ye servants of the most high God, go ye forth, and come. And immediately Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, went out from the midst of the fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the nobles, and the magistrates, and the judges, and the great men of the king, being gathered together, considered these men, that the fire had no power on their bodies, and that not a hair of their head had been singed, nor their garments altered, nor the smell of the fire had passed on them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Nabuchodonosor breaking forth, said: Blessed be the God of them, to wit, of Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, who hath sent his angel, and delivered his servants that believed in him: and they changed the king’s word, and delivered up their bodies, that they might not serve nor adore any god except their own God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By me, therefore, this decree is made: That every people, tribe, and tongue, which shall speak blasphemy against the God of Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, shall be destroyed, and their houses laid waste: for there is no other God that can save in this manner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king promoted Sidrach, Misach, and Abdenago, in the province of Babylon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nabuchodonosor, the king, to all peoples, nations, and tongues, that dwell in all the earth, peace be multiplied unto you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The most high God hath wrought signs and wonders towards me. It hath seemed good to me, therefore, to publish

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: His signs, because they are great: and his wonders, because they are mighty: and his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and his power to all generations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I, Nabuchodonosor, was at rest in my house, and flourishing in my palace:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I saw a dream that affrighted me: and my thoughts in my bed, and the visions of my head, troubled me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then I set forth a decree, that all the wise men of Babylon should be brought in before me, and that they should shew me the interpretation of the dream.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then came in the diviners, the wise men, the Chaldeans, and the soothsayers, and I told the dream before them: but they did not shew me the interpretation thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till their colleague, Daniel, came in before me, whose name is Baltassar, according to the name of my god, who hath in him the spirit of the holy gods: and I told the dream before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Baltassar, prince of the diviners, because I know that thou hast in thee the spirit of the holy gods, and that no secret is impossible to thee, tell me the visions of my dreams that I have seen, and the interpretation of them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This was the vision of my head in my bed: I saw, and behold a tree in the midst of the earth, and the height thereof was exceeding great.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tree was great and strong, and the height thereof reached unto heaven: the sight thereof was even to the ends of all the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Its leaves were most beautiful, and its fruit exceeding much: and in it was food for all: under it dwelt cattle and beasts, and in the branches thereof the fowls of the air had their abode: and all flesh did eat of it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I saw in the vision of my head upon my bed, and behold a watcher, and a holy one came down from heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He cried aloud, and said thus: Cut down the tree, and chop off the branches thereof: shake off its leaves, and scatter its fruits: let the beasts fly away that are under it, and the birds from its branches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nevertheless, leave the stump of its roots in the earth, and let it be tied with a band of iron and of brass, among the grass, that is without, and let it be wet with the dew of heaven, and let its portion be with the wild beasts in the grass of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let his heart be changed from man’s, and let a beast’s heart be given him: and let seven times pass over him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This is the decree by the sentence of the watchers, and the word and demand of the holy ones: till the living know, that the most High ruleth in the kingdom of men: and he will give it to whomsoever it shall please him, and he will appoint the basest man over it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I, king Nabuchodonosor, saw this dream: thou, therefore, O Baltassar, tell me quickly the interpretation: for all the wise men of my kingdom are not able to declare the meaning of it to me: but thou art able, because the spirit of the holy gods is in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Daniel, whose name was Baltassar, began silently to think within himself for about one hour: and his thought troubled him. But the king answering, said: Baltassar, let not the dream and the interpretation thereof trouble thee. Baltassar answered, and said: My lord, the dream be to them that hate thee, and the interpretation thereof to thy enemies.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The tree which thou sawest, which was high and strong, whose height reached to the skies, and the sight thereof into all the earth:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the branches thereof were most beautiful, and its fruit exceeding much, and in it was food for all, under which the beasts of the field dwelt, and the birds of the air had their abode in its branches.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is thou, O king, who art grown great, and become mighty: for thy greatness hath grown, and hath reached to heaven, and thy power unto the ends of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas the king saw a watcher, and a holy one come down from heaven, and say: Cut down the tree, and destroy it, but leave the stump of the roots thereof in the earth, and let it be bound with iron and brass, among the grass without, and let it be sprinkled with the dew of heaven, and let his feeding be with the wild beasts, till seven times pass over him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This is the interpretation of the sentence of the most High, which is come upon my lord, the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They shall cast thee out from among men, and thy dwelling shall be with cattle, and with wild beasts, and thou shalt eat grass, as an ox, and shalt be wet with the dew of heaven: and seven times shall pass over thee, till thou know that the most High ruleth over the kingdom of men, and giveth it to whomsoever he will.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But whereas he commanded, that the stump of the roots thereof, that is, of the tree, should be left: thy kingdom shall remain to thee, after thou shalt have known that power is from heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, O king, let my counsel be acceptable to thee, and redeem thou thy sins with alms, and thy iniquities with works of mercy to the poor: perhaps he will forgive thy offences.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All these things came upon king Nabuchodonosor.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the end of twelve months he was walking in the palace of Babylon.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king answered, and said: Is not this the great Babylon, which I have built, to be the seat of the kingdom, by the strength of my power, and in the glory of my excellence?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And while the word was yet in the king’s mouth, a voice came down from heaven: To thee, O king Nabuchodonosor, it is said: Thy kingdom shall pass from thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall cast thee out from among men, and thy dwelling shall be with cattle and wild beasts: thou shalt eat grass like an ox, and seven times shall pass over thee, till thou know that the most High ruleth in the kingdom of men, and giveth it to whomsoever he will.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The same hour the word was fulfilled upon Nabuchodonosor, and he was driven away from among men, and did eat grass, like an ox, and his body was wet with the dew of heaven: till his hairs grew like the feathers of eagles, and his nails like birds’ claws.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now at the end of the days, I, Nabuchodonosor, lifted up my eyes to heaven, and my sense was restored to me: and I blessed the most High, and I praised and glorified him that liveth for ever: for his power is an everlasting power, and his kingdom is to all generations.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all the inhabitants of the earth are reputed as nothing before him: for he doth according to his will, as well with the powers of heaven, as among the inhabitants of the earth: and there is none that can resist his hand, and say to him: Why hast thou done it?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At the same time my sense returned to me, and I came to the honour and glory of my kingdom: and my shape returned to me: and my nobles, and my magistrates, sought for me, and I was restored to my kingdom: and greater majesty was added to me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore I, Nabuchodonosor, do now praise, and magnify, and glorify the King of heaven: because all his works are true, and his ways judgments, and them that walk in pride he is able to abase.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Baltasar, the king, made a great feast for a thousand of his nobles: and every one drank according to his age.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And being now drunk, he commanded that they should bring the vessels of gold and silver, which Nabuchodonosor, his father, had brought away out of the temple, that was in Jerusalem, that the king and his nobles, and his wives, and his concubines, might drink in them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then were the golden and silver vessels brought, which he had brought away out of the temple that was in Jerusalem: and the king and his nobles, his wives, and his concubines, drank in them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: They drank wine, and praised their gods of gold, and of silver, of brass, of iron, and of wood, and of stone.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the same hour there appeared fingers, as it were of the hand of a man, writing over against the candlestick, upon the surface of the wall of the king’s palace: and the king beheld the joints of the hand that wrote.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then was the king’s countenance changed, and his thoughts troubled him: and the joints of his loins were loosed, and his knees struck one against the other.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king cried out aloud to bring in the wise men, the Chaldeans, and the soothsayers. And the king spoke, and said to the wise men of Babylon: Whosoever shall read this writing, and shall make known to me the interpretation thereof, shall be clothed with purple, and shall have a golden chain on his neck, and shall be the third man in my kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then came in all the king’s wise men, but they could neither read the writing, nor declare the interpretation to the king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherewith king Baltasar was much troubled, and his countenance was changed: and his nobles also were troubled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the queen, on occasion of what had happened to the king, and his nobles, came into the banquet-house: and she spoke, and said: O king, live for ever: let not thy thoughts trouble thee, neither let thy countenance be changed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a man in thy kingdom that hath the spirit of the holy gods in him: and in the days of thy father knowledge and wisdom were found in him: for king Nabuchodonosor, thy father, appointed him prince of the wise men, enchanters, Chaldeans, and soothsayers, thy father, I say, O king:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because a greater spirit, and knowledge, and understanding, and interpretation of dreams, and shewing of secrets, and resolving of difficult things, were found in him, that is, in Daniel: whom the king named Baltassar. Now, therefore, let Daniel be called for, and he will tell the interpretation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then Daniel was brought in before the king. And the king spoke, and said to him: Art thou Daniel, of the children of the captivity of Juda, whom my father, the king, brought out of Judea?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have heard of thee, that thou hast the spirit of the gods, and excellent knowledge, and understanding, and wisdom are found in thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And now the wise men, the magicians, have come in before me, to read this writing, and shew me the interpretation thereof; and they could not declare to me the meaning of this writing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But I have heard of thee, that thou canst interpret obscure things, and resolve difficult things: now if thou art able to read the writing, and to shew me the interpretation thereof, thou shalt be clothed with purple, and shalt have a chain of gold about thy neck, and shalt be the third prince in my kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: To which Daniel made answer, and said before the king: thy rewards be to thyself, and the gifts of thy house give to another: but the writing I will read to thee, O king, and shew thee the interpretation thereof.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: O king, the most high God gave to Nabuchodonosor, thy father, a kingdom, and greatness, and glory, and honour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And for the greatness that he gave to him, all people, tribes, and languages trembled, and were afraid of him: whom he would, he slew: and whom he would, he destroyed: and whom he would, he set up: and whom he would, he brought down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But when his heart was lifted up, and his spirit hardened unto pride, he was put down from the throne of his kingdom, and his glory was taken away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he was driven out from the sons of men, and his heart was made like the beasts, and his dwelling was with the wild asses, and he did eat grass like an ox, and his body was wet with the dew of heaven: till he knew that the most High ruled in the kingdom of men, and that he will set over it whomsoever it shall please him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Thou also, his son, O Baltasar, hast not humbled thy heart, whereas thou knewest all these things:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But hast lifted thyself up against the Lord of heaven: and the vessels of his house have been brought before thee: and thou, and thy nobles, and thy wives, and thy concubines, have drunk wine in them: and thou hast praised the gods of silver, and of gold, and of brass, of iron, and of wood, and of stone, that neither see, nor hear, nor feel: but the God who hath thy breath in his hand, and all thy ways, thou hast not glorified.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore, he hath sent the part of the hand which hath written this that is set down.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this is the writing that is written: MANE, THECEL, PHARES.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And this is the interpretation of the word. MANE: God hath numbered thy kingdom, and hath finished it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: THECEL: thou art weighed in the balance, and art found wanting.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: PHARES: thy kingdom is divided, and is given to the Medes and Persians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then by the king’s command, Daniel was clothed with purple, and a chain of gold was put about his neck: and it was proclaimed of him that he had power as the third man in the kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The same night Baltasar, the Chaldean king, was slain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Darius, the Mede, succeeded to the kingdom, being threescore and two years old.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It seemed good to Darius, and he appointed over the kingdom a hundred and twenty governors, to be over his whole kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And three princes over them of whom Daniel was one: that the governors might give an account to them, and the king might have no trouble.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Daniel excelled all the princes, and governors: because a greater spirit of God was in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king thought to set him over all the kingdom; whereupon the princes, and the governors, sought to find occasion against Daniel, with regard to the king: and they could find no cause, nor suspicion, because he was faithful, and no fault, nor suspicion was found in him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then these men said: We shall not find any occasion against this Daniel, unless perhaps concerning the law of his God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the princes, and the governors, craftily suggested to the king, and spoke thus unto him: King Darius, live for ever:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the princes of the kingdom, the magistrates, and governors, the senators, and judges, have consulted together, that an imperial decree, and an edict be published: That whosoever shall ask any petition of any god, or man, for thirty days, but of thee, O king, shall be cast into the den of the lions.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, therefore, O king, confirm the sentence, and sign the decree: that what is decreed by the Medes and Persians may not be altered, nor any man be allowed to transgress it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: So king Darius set forth the decree, and established it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now, when Daniel knew this, that is to say, that the law was made, he went into his house: and opening the windows in his upper chamber towards Jerusalem, he knelt down three times a day, and adored and gave thanks before his God, as he had been accustomed to do before.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore those men carefully watching him, found Daniel praying and making supplication to his God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they came and spoke to the king concerning the edict: O king, hast thou not decreed, that every man that should make a request to any of the gods, or men, for thirty days, but to thyself, O king, should be cast into the den of the lions? And the king answered them, saying: The word is true, according to the decree of the Medes and Persians, which it is not lawful to violate.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then they answered, and said before the king: Daniel, who is of the children of the captivity of Juda, hath not regarded thy law, nor the decree that thou hast made: but three times a day he maketh his prayer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now when the king had heard these words, he was very much grieved, and in behalf of Daniel he set his heart to deliver him, and even till sunset he laboured to save him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But those men perceiving the king’s design, said to him: Know thou, O king, that the law of the Medes and Persians is, that no decree which the king hath made, may be altered.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king commanded, and they brought Daniel, and cast him into the den of the lions. And the king said to Daniel: Thy God, whom thou always servest, he will deliver thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a stone was brought, and laid upon the mouth of the den: which the king sealed with his own ring, and with the ring of his nobles, that nothing should be done against Daniel.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the king went away to his house, and laid himself down without taking supper, and meat was not set before him, and even sleep departed from him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then the king rising very early in the morning, went in haste to the lions’ den:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And coming near to the den, cried with a lamentable voice to Daniel, and said to him: Daniel, servant of the living God, hath thy God, whom thou servest always, been able, thinkest thou, to deliver thee from the lions?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And Daniel answering the king, said: O king, live for ever:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My God hath sent his angel, and hath shut up the mouths of the lions, and they have not hurt me: forasmuch as before him justice hath been found in me: yea, and before thee, O king, I have done no offence.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then was the king exceeding glad for him, and he commanded that Daniel should be taken out of the den: and Daniel was taken out of the den, and no hurt was found in him, because he believed in his God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And by the king’s commandment, those men were brought that had accused Daniel: and they were cast into the lions’ den, they and their children, and their wives: and they did not reach the bottom of the den, before the lions caught them, and broke all their bones in pieces.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Then king Darius wrote to all people, tribes, and languages, dwelling in the whole earth: PEACE be multiplied unto you.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is decreed by me, that in all my empire and my kingdom, all men dread and fear the God of Daniel. For he is the living and eternal God for ever: and his kingdom shall not be destroyed, and his power shall be for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He is the deliverer, and saviour, doing signs and wonders in heaven, and in earth: who hath delivered Daniel out of the lions’ den.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now Daniel continued unto the reign of Darius, and the reign of Cyrus, the Persian.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the first year of Baltasar, king of Babylon, Daniel saw a dream: and the vision of his head was upon his bed: and writing the dream, he comprehended it in a few words: and relating the sum of it in short, he said:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I saw in my vision by night, and behold the four winds of the heaven strove upon the great sea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And four great beasts, different one from another, came up out of the sea.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The first was like a lioness, and had the wings of an eagle: I beheld till her wings were plucked off, and she was lifted up from the earth, and stood upon her feet as a man, and the heart of a man was given to her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And behold another beast, like a bear, stood up on one side: and there were three rows in the mouth thereof, and in the teeth thereof, and thus they said to it: Arise, devour much flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this I beheld, and lo, another like a leopard, and it had upon it four wings, as of a fowl, and the beast had four heads, and power was given to it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this I beheld in the vision of the night, and lo, a fourth beast, terrible and wonderful, and exceeding strong, it had great iron teeth, eating and breaking in pieces, and treading down the rest with its feet: and it was unlike to the other beasts which I had seen before it, and had ten horns.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I considered the horns, and behold another little horn sprung out of the midst of them: and three of the first horns were plucked up at the presence thereof: and behold eyes like the eyes of a man were in this horn, and a mouth speaking great things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I beheld till thrones were placed, and the ancient of days sat: his garment was white as snow, and the hair of his head like clean wool: his throne like flames of fire: the wheels of it like a burning fire.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A swift stream of fire issued forth from before him: thousands of thousands ministered to him, and ten thousand times a hundred thousand stood before him: the judgment sat, and the books were opened.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I beheld, because of the voice of the great words which that horn spoke: and I saw that the beast was slain, and the body thereof was destroyed, and given to the fire to be burnt:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that the power of the other beasts was taken away: and that times of life were appointed them for a time, and a time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I beheld, therefore, in the vision of the night, and lo, one like the Son of man came with the clouds of heaven, and he came even to the ancient of days: and they presented him before him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he gave him power, and glory, and a kingdom: and all peoples, tribes, and tongues shall serve him: his power is an everlasting power that shall not be taken away: and his kingdom that shall not be destroyed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: My spirit trembled; I, Daniel, was affrighted at these things, and the visions of my head troubled me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I went near to one of them that stood by, and asked the truth of him concerning all these things, and he told me the interpretation of the words, and instructed me:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These four great beasts, are four kingdoms, which shall arise out of the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But the saints of the most high God shall take the kingdom: and they shall possess the kingdom for ever and ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: After this I would diligently learn concerning the fourth beast, which was very different from all, and exceeding terrible: his teeth and claws were of iron: he devoured and broke in pieces, and the rest he stamped upon with his feet:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And concerning the ten horns that he had on his head: and concerning the other that came up, before which three horns fell: and of that horn that had eyes, and a mouth speaking great things, and was greater than the rest.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I beheld, and lo, that horn made war against the saints, and prevailed over them,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Till the ancient of days came and gave judgment to the saints of the most High, and the time came, and the saints obtained the kingdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And thus he said: The fourth beast shall be the fourth kingdom upon earth, which shall be greater than all the kingdoms, and shall devour the whole earth, and shall tread it down, and break it in pieces.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the ten horns of the same kingdom, shall be ten kings: and another shall rise up after them, and he shall be mightier than the former, and he shall bring down three kings.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he shall speak words against the High One, and shall crush the saints of the most High: and he shall think himself able to change times and laws, and they shall be delivered into his hand until a time, and times, and half a time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And a judgment shall sit, that his power may be taken away, and be broken in pieces, and perish even to the end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And that the kingdom, and power, and the greatness of the kingdom, under the whole heaven, may be given to the people of the saints of the most High: whose kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and all kings shall serve him, and shall obey him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Hitherto is the end of the word. I, Daniel, was much troubled with my thoughts, and my countenance was changed in me: but I kept the word in my heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the third year of the reign of king Baltasar, a vision appeared to me. I, Daniel, after what I had seen in the beginning,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Saw in my vision when I was in the castle of Susa, which is in the province of Elam: and I saw in the vision that I was over the gate of Ulai.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I lifted up my eyes, and saw: and behold a ram stood before the water, having two high horns, and one higher than the other, and growing up. Afterward

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I saw the ram pushing with his horns against the west, and against the north, and against the south: and no beasts could withstand him, nor be delivered out of his hand: and he did according to his own will, and became great.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I understood: and behold a he goat came from the west on the face of the whole earth, and he touched not the ground, and the he goat had a notable horn between his eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he went up to the ram that had the horns, which I had seen standing before the gate, and he ran towards him in the force of his strength.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he was come near the ram, he was enraged against him, and struck the ram: and broke his two horns, and the ram could not withstand him: and when he had cast him down on the ground, he stamped upon him, and none could deliver the ram out of his hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the he goat became exceeding great: and when he was grown, the great horn was broken, and there came up four horns under it towards the four winds of heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And out of one of them came forth a little horn: and it became great against the south, and against the east, and against the strength.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was magnified even unto the strength of heaven: and it threw down of the strength, and of the stars, and trod upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it was magnified even to the prince of the strength: and it took away from him the continual sacrifice, and cast down the place of his sanctuary.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And strength was given him against the continual sacrifice, because of sins: and truth shall be cast down on the ground, and he shall do and shall prosper.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I heard one of the saints speaking, and one saint said to another I know not to whom, that was speaking: How long shall be the vision, concerning the continual sacrifice, and the sin of the desolation that is made: and the sanctuary, and the strength be trodden under foot?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to him: Unto evening and morning two thousand three hundred days: and the sanctuary shall be cleansed.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it came to pass when I, Daniel, saw the vision, and sought the meaning, that behold there stood before me as it were the appearance of a man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I heard the voice of a man between Ulai: and he called, and said: Gabriel, make this man to understand the vision.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he came, and stood near where I stood: and when he was come, I fell on my face, trembling, and he said to me: Understand, O son of man, for in the time of the end the vision shall be fulfilled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when he spoke to me, I fell flat on the ground: and he touched me, and set me upright.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And he said to me: I will shew thee what things are to come to pass in the end of the malediction: for the time hath its end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The ram, which thou sawest with horns, is the king of the Medes and Persians.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the he goat, is the king of the Greeks, and the great horn that was between his eyes, the same is the first king.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But whereas when that was broken, there arose up four for it, four kings shall rise up of his nation, but not with his strength.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And after their reign, when iniquities shall be grown up, there shall arise a king of a shameless face, and understanding dark sentences.
